context: Items where Gemini passes disagreed. Review and classify.

items.text: He'd skipped supper???too excited about the red sky to sit still for bread and cheese. Now the hunger felt distant, like a complaint from someone else's body.
items.gemini_reasoning: Describes Dane's emotional state (excitement) leading to an action (skipping supper) and his subjective physical sensation (distant hunger).

items.text: He thought about the apple he'd hidden under his pillow. Green, from the tree by the well, stolen before it was ripe because he couldn't wait. It would still be there when this was over. If this was ever over.
items.gemini_reasoning: Reveals Dane's impatience ('couldn't wait') and a glimmer of hope/uncertainty about the future ('If this was ever over').

items.text: A man of logic, of scripture, of the ordered universe that God had created and organized into comprehensible laws.
items.gemini_reasoning: Describes Milutin Tesla's philosophical and religious convictions as presented in the narrative, potentially an embellished or specific interpretation for the series.

items.text: He spoke four languages. He could parse the Greek of the apostles and the Latin of the church fathers.
items.gemini_reasoning: Verifiable historical facts about Milutin Tesla's linguistic and scholarly abilities.

items.text: Tonight, language had failed him. He rocked back and forth, clutching his prayer book, begging the sky for mercy in a voice that cracked and broke like kindling.
items.gemini_reasoning: Describes Milutin's subjective state of distress, inability to cope, and his desperate actions.

items.text: Dane watched his father weep. Then he lifted his eyes to the sky. He did not see judgment. He saw machinery.
items.gemini_reasoning: Highlights Dane's observational and detached reaction to his father's distress, and his unique, analytical interpretation of the sky phenomenon.

items.text: He did not see judgment. He saw machinery.
items.gemini_reasoning: A significant discovery or interpretation by Dane about the nature of the sky phenomenon, diverging from the common fearful interpretation.

items.text: Most of the villagers had fled indoors. The ones who remained outside were on their knees, crossing themselves, certain the end had come.
items.gemini_reasoning: Describes the villagers' collective fear and belief that the apocalypse was upon them.

items.text: Behind the red curtain, something else flickered. White lines. Faint, geometric, crossing the sky in perfect triangles- triangles that make hexagons and hexagons that made??? a grid. The white lattice pulsed and snapped. The lines tried to connect, tried to form a cage around the valley, but the links kept breaking. Threads of light would reach toward each other, strain, then shatter into sparks that rained upward instead of down.
items.gemini_reasoning: Dane's discovery of a hidden, structured, and active phenomenon behind the initial red light, revealing a complex 'grid' or 'lattice'.

items.text: He looked down at his hands. The red light passed through his skin. His fingers had gone translucent. He could see the dark architecture of his bones, the black web of veins branching beneath the surface.
items.gemini_reasoning: Detailed visual description of the effect of the light on Dane's hands.

items.text: His circulation had become something denser than plasma. Something that remembered iron. The iron in his blood was pulling upward. Not a metaphor. Not imagination. He could feel each red cell straining toward the sky, toward whatever hung behind the red curtain, as if his own biology had chosen a new loyalty.
items.gemini_reasoning: Describes a unique and fictional physiological response within Dane's body, where his iron-rich blood is literally being drawn upwards, presented as a concrete event within the narrative.

items.text: His skeleton was warming from the inside. Dane was being cooked.
items.gemini_reasoning: Uses a powerful metaphor ('Dane was being cooked') to convey the intense internal heat he experiences.

items.text: Any other child would have run to a parent. Would have buried his face in his father's coat and wept, but Dane stood up. He balanced on the peak of the barn roof. He spread his fingers against the red light and watched the bones glow through the flesh. He was testing himself. Measuring how much radiation his body could absorb before something broke.
items.gemini_reasoning: Highlights Dane's extraordinary and unconventional response to danger, showing his stoicism, scientific curiosity, and self-testing nature.

items.text: Then he looked toward the house. The windows were dark. All except one. The nursery was glowing. But not red. The light pouring from that window was white. Blinding, clinical white???the color of an operating theater, the color of a laboratory lamp focused on a specimen.
items.gemini_reasoning: Dane discovers a new, contrasting phenomenon: a white, clinical light emanating from the nursery, distinct from the red light in the sky.

items.text: The ground brittle beneath him as if the earth itself had been dehydrated???every drop of moisture pulled upward, sacrificed to the column of white light drilling into his brother's nursery.
items.gemini_reasoning: Describes a fictional physical effect on the environment, where moisture is drawn upward and sacrificed to the white light, presented as a fact within the narrative.

items.text: The light above the valley was not a glow. It was a curtain... The fabric rippled. It moved in waves, responding to a wind that didn't touch the trees below.
items.gemini_reasoning: Visual descriptions of the sky phenomenon's movement and appearance.
